hero-title: What is Augmented Intelligence (AI That Helps You)?
lead: Augmented intelligence just means you and the machine work together. The machine is fast. You make the smart choices, so you stay in charge. The cool part isn't the AI itself. The cool part is the extra time you get, and what you choose to do with it.

Augmented intelligence pairs your judgment with the machine's speed, and it keeps you in charge the whole time. The point isn't really the AI. It's the time it hands back to you, and what you do with that time.
Augmented intelligence pairs your judgment with the machine's speed — and keeps you in charge. The point isn't the AI. It's what you do with the time it gives back.
Augmented intelligence pairs your judgment with the machine's speed, and keeps you in charge. The point isn't the AI; it's what you do with the reclaimed time.
Augmented intelligence: your judgment plus machine speed, you in charge. The point is the time reclaimed, not the AI.
enduring-idea: The one big thing to remember: AI is a tool, and you are the one who tells it what to do. Give it the boring, bulky work. Keep the thinking for yourself. You stay in charge of every choice that really matters.
The one idea to keep: AI is a tool that you direct. Hand it the heavy volume of work, and keep the judgment for yourself. Stay in charge of every decision that matters.
The one idea to keep: AI is a tool you direct. Hand it the volume; keep the judgment. Stay in charge of every decision that matters.
The one idea to keep: AI is a tool you direct. Hand off the volume, keep the judgment, and own every decision that matters.
Keep this: AI is a tool you direct. Offload volume, retain judgment, own the decisions.
example-intro: Here's the whole idea, shown with one real job. Meet Maria. She runs a busy clinic, and she has to give the board a report on 400 patient comments by Friday. That's a lot. Below, you can try each way of getting the job done and see what happens.
Here's the whole idea in one job. Maria runs a busy clinic, and she owes the board a report on 400 patient comments by Friday. Try each different way of getting it done:

explorable-takeaway: Notice that only the last button fills up both bars. That's the whole idea in one picture: the machine handles the big pile of work, and you keep the thinking — like what matters most, the mistakes only a person would spot, and the final say. The middle button is "AI that replaces you." It's fast, but nobody is checking it, so its confident mistakes slip right through.

Only the last button fills both bars, and that's the whole idea in one picture: the machine handles the volume, and you keep the judgment (the priorities, the mistakes only a person would catch, the final call). "AI that replaces you" is the middle button: fast, but with no one watching, its confident errors sail straight through.

"AI that replaces you" is the middle button: fast, but with no one in the loop, its confident errors sail straight through.

Only the last button fills both bars — the whole idea in one picture: the machine handles volume, you keep judgment (priorities, the mistakes only a person catches, the final call). The middle button is "AI that replaces you": fast, but with no one in the loop, its confident errors sail through.

Only the last button fills both bars: machine handles volume, you keep judgment (priorities, human-only catches, final call). The middle button — "AI replaces you" — is fast but unsupervised, so confident errors pass through.
recall-question: When you give a job to AI, which part do you keep, and which part do you let go of?
When you hand work to AI, what should you keep, and what should you give away?
When you hand work to AI, what do you keep and what do you give away?
Handing work to AI: what do you keep, and what do you give away?
AI handoff: keep what, give away what?
recall-answer: Give away the volume — that's the repeated, mechanical work a machine does well. Keep the judgment — that's the context, what matters most, the right-and-wrong calls, and the final say. Those stay with you.
Give away the volume — the repetitive, mechanical work. Keep the judgment — the context, the priorities, the ethics, and the final call.
Give away the volume — the repetitive, mechanical work. Keep the judgment — context, priorities, ethics, and the final call.
Give away the volume (repetitive, mechanical work). Keep the judgment: context, priorities, ethics, the final call.
Give away volume (repetitive, mechanical work). Keep judgment: context, priorities, ethics, final call.

tryit-intro: This idea only really clicks when you try it on something real from your own life. So pick one task you have to do this week:

tryit-step-1: Write down the task in just one short line.
Write the task down in one line.
Write the task in one line.
State the task in one line.
Name the task in one line.
tryit-step-2: Now split that task into two piles. One pile is the volume — the repeated, mechanical work that a machine could draft, sort, or search for you. The other pile is the judgment — the parts that need your own context, your sense of right and wrong, your taste, or your final say.
Split it into two parts. Volume is the repetitive, mechanical work that could be drafted, sorted, or searched. Judgment is the part that needs your context, ethics, taste, or final call.
Split it into volume (repetitive, mechanical — could be drafted, sorted, or searched) and judgment (needs your context, ethics, taste, or final call).
Split it into volume (repetitive, mechanical — draftable, sortable, searchable) and judgment (needs your context, ethics, taste, or final call).
Split into volume (mechanical: draft/sort/search) and judgment (your context, ethics, taste, final call).
tryit-step-3: Now decide which of the volume parts you would hand over to AI. Then mark the exact moment where you would step back in to check the work, just like Maria did with her report.
Decide which volume parts you would hand to AI — and pick the exact point where you'd step back in to verify, the way Maria did.
Decide which volume parts you'd hand to AI — and the exact point where you'd stay in the loop to verify, the way Maria did.
Decide which volume parts to hand to AI — and the exact point where you'd stay in the loop to verify, as Maria did.
Decide which volume to delegate to AI, and the exact checkpoint where you verify — as Maria did.
tryit-closer: That split is the skill — there isn't a secret extra step. Do it five times this week, and soon it stops feeling like an exercise. It just becomes a habit you reach for without thinking.
That split is the skill. Do it five times this week, and it stops being an exercise and becomes a habit.

That split is the skill. Do it five times this week and it shifts from exercise to habit.
The split is the skill. Five reps this week turns exercise into habit.
continue-intro: That's the whole idea, really. Where to next? You could read about why this is worth your time, or you could look at the three pillars that the whole idea rests on.
